front_text: Abortion is defined as pregnancy loss before 20 weeks of gestation, based on the last menstrual period (LMP), and includes both spontaneous (natural) and induced (intentional) losses.
front_html: Abortion is defined as pregnancy loss before {{c1::20}} weeks of gestation, based on the last menstrual period (LMP), and includes both {{c1::spontaneous}} (natural) and {{c1::induced}} (intentional) losses.
vignette: You are seeing Abortion Annie, a 28-year-old woman presenting to your outpatient obstetrics clinic after a confirmed intrauterine pregnancy at 8 weeks by last menstrual period (LMP). She reports vaginal bleeding and lower abdominal cramping that began 24 hours ago, and she is asking about her options and what her diagnosis means. You explain that pregnancy loss before 20 weeks gestation is classified as an abortion, while loss at or after 20 weeks is classified as a fetal demise or stillbirth. Her loss at 8 weeks falls within the early abortion category, defined as loss before 12 weeks, as opposed to late abortion, which occurs between 12 and 20 weeks. As a PA, always anchor your classification of pregnancy loss to gestational age by LMP, because the 20-week threshold determines whether you are managing an abortion, a fetal demise, or a preterm delivery, and this distinction drives every subsequent clinical and legal decision.
teaching_case:
Patient Presentation
You are seeing Abortion Annie, a 28-year-old woman presenting to your outpatient obstetrics clinic with vaginal bleeding and pelvic cramping at 8 weeks gestation by LMP, requesting clarification of her diagnosis and options. She has a confirmed intrauterine pregnancy on prior ultrasound and denies fever, shoulder pain, or syncope. She asks whether her situation is considered a miscarriage, a stillbirth, or something else, and whether it was caused by anything she did. You explain that abortion is defined as any pregnancy loss before 20 weeks gestation by LMP, encompassing both spontaneous (natural) and induced (intentional) losses, and that her presentation at 8 weeks is classified as an early abortion (before 12 weeks), distinct from a late abortion (12 to 20 weeks) or a fetal demise (at or after 20 weeks).

Physical Examination
On examination, her vital signs are stable with no signs of hemodynamic compromise. Speculum examination reveals a small amount of blood in the vaginal vault with a closed cervical os, which is consistent with a threatened or incomplete abortion depending on subsequent imaging. Bimanual examination confirms a uterus consistent with early pregnancy without adnexal tenderness or mass, making ectopic pregnancy less likely. Pertinent negatives include absence of peritoneal signs and no cervical motion tenderness.

Workup and Diagnosis
You order a transvaginal ultrasound, which is the cornerstone of abortion evaluation, to assess for gestational sac location, fetal cardiac activity, and uterine contents. A quantitative β-hCG is drawn; in a viable pregnancy, β-hCG should double every 48 hours, and a plateau or decline raises concern for miscarriage or ectopic pregnancy. Blood type and Rh status are checked to determine whether Rho(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM) is indicated to prevent Rh sensitization. The classification of the abortion as spontaneous versus induced and early versus late is confirmed by gestational age on ultrasound correlated with LMP.

Treatment
Management is tailored to the type and classification of abortion identified. For a spontaneous early abortion, options include expectant management (allowing natural passage of products of conception), medical management with misoprostol to induce uterine contractions, or surgical management with suction curettage or D&C for retained products or heavy bleeding. For an induced abortion, the method selected depends on gestational age: medical abortion with mifepristone plus misoprostol is used up to 7 to 9 weeks LMP, while surgical options are used for later gestations. Rho(D) immune globulin is administered to all Rh-negative patients regardless of abortion type.

Follow Up and Monitoring
You schedule follow-up with serial β-hCG or repeat transvaginal ultrasound to confirm complete passage of products of conception and resolution of pregnancy tissue. You provide emotional support and preconception counseling, including discussion of future pregnancy planning and any modifiable risk factors.

PA EOR Board Pearls
1. Abortion is defined as pregnancy loss before 20 weeks gestation by LMP; loss at or after 20 weeks is classified as fetal demise or stillbirth.
2. Early abortion occurs before 12 weeks; late abortion occurs between 12 and 20 weeks; a fetus born alive between 20 and 36 weeks 6 days is a preterm delivery.
3. Spontaneous abortion is noninduced pregnancy loss; therapeutic abortion is induced due to maternal health risk or fetal nonviability; elective abortion is induced for personal or social reasons.
4. Always check blood type and Rh status in any abortion; administer RhoGAM to all Rh-negative patients to prevent isoimmunization.
5. Transvaginal ultrasound is the gold standard imaging modality for evaluating any type of abortion and confirming gestational age and uterine contents.

front_text: Most spontaneous abortions occur in the first trimester (before 12 weeks), and approximately 60% are due to chromosomal abnormalities, usually trisomy 16.
front_html: Most spontaneous abortions occur in the {{c1::first trimester}} (before 12 weeks), and approximately {{c1::60}}% are due to {{c1::chromosomal}} abnormalities, usually {{c1::trisomy 16}}.
vignette: You are seeing Spontaneous Sam, a 37-year-old woman with a history of two prior spontaneous abortions presenting to your urgent care clinic at 9 weeks gestation by LMP with vaginal bleeding and pelvic cramping. She has a history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes, smokes half a pack per day, and her partner is 47 years old. Transvaginal ultrasound reveals an absent fetal cardiac activity with a gestational sac present, and her quantitative β-hCG has plateaued over 48 hours rather than doubling. You check her blood type and Rh status and find she is Rh-negative, making her a candidate for Rho(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM). As a PA, always evaluate any patient with two or more consecutive spontaneous abortions for recurrent abortion by ordering parental karyotyping, thyroid function tests, and antiphospholipid antibody testing, because identifying a treatable underlying cause is the most impactful intervention you can offer.
teaching_case:
Patient Presentation
You are seeing Spontaneous Sam, a 37-year-old woman presenting to your urgent care clinic at 9 weeks gestation by LMP with vaginal bleeding and pelvic cramping and a history of two prior spontaneous abortions. She has poorly controlled type 2 diabetes, smokes actively, and her partner is 47 years old, all of which are recognized risk factors for spontaneous abortion. She denies fever, shoulder pain, or syncope, and her vital signs are stable. Approximately 60% of spontaneous abortions are caused by chromosomal abnormalities, most commonly trisomy 16, and most occur in the first trimester.

Physical Examination
On speculum examination you identify blood in the vaginal vault with a closed cervical os, consistent with a threatened or missed abortion. Bimanual examination reveals a uterus appropriate for gestational age without adnexal tenderness or mass, making ectopic pregnancy less likely. Pertinent negatives include no cervical motion tenderness and no peritoneal signs. The closed os in the setting of confirmed fetal demise on ultrasound is consistent with a missed abortion.

Workup and Diagnosis
You order a transvaginal ultrasound, which demonstrates absent fetal cardiac activity and confirms intrauterine pregnancy, ruling out ectopic pregnancy. A quantitative β-hCG drawn 48 hours apart shows a plateau rather than the expected doubling, confirming nonviable pregnancy. Blood type and Rh status are checked; she is Rh-negative, so Rho(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM) is indicated. Because this is her third pregnancy loss, you initiate a recurrent abortion workup including parental karyotyping, thyroid function tests, and antiphospholipid antibody testing to evaluate for genetic, endocrine, and autoimmune causes including antiphospholipid antibody syndrome and SLE.

Treatment
You discuss three management options with the patient: expectant management (allowing natural passage of products of conception with monitoring), medical management with misoprostol (a prostaglandin that induces uterine contractions), or surgical management with suction curettage or D&C, which is indicated for retained products of conception or heavy bleeding. She opts for medical management, and you administer misoprostol with instructions for home use and return precautions. Rho(D) immune globulin is administered given her Rh-negative status.

Follow Up and Monitoring
You schedule follow-up with serial β-hCG and repeat transvaginal ultrasound to confirm complete passage of products of conception and return of β-hCG to nonpregnant levels. You provide emotional support, preconception counseling, and review results of the recurrent abortion workup to guide management of future pregnancies.

PA EOR Board Pearls
1. Approximately 60% of spontaneous abortions are caused by chromosomal abnormalities; the most common specific chromosomal cause is trisomy 16.
2. Risk factors for spontaneous abortion include advanced maternal age (≥35 years), paternal age ≥45, smoking, alcohol, cocaine use, and poorly controlled diabetes, thyroid disease, and hypertension.
3. Recurrent abortion is defined as 2 to 3 or more consecutive spontaneous abortions; always evaluate with parental karyotyping, thyroid function tests, and antiphospholipid antibody testing.
4. In a viable pregnancy, β-hCG doubles every 48 hours; a plateau or decline indicates miscarriage or ectopic pregnancy.
5. Transvaginal ultrasound is the cornerstone of abortion evaluation; key findings include empty uterus, abnormal gestational sac, and absent fetal cardiac activity.
6. Always check Rh status and administer RhoGAM to Rh-negative patients with any pregnancy loss to prevent isoimmunization.
7. Causes of recurrent abortion include parental chromosomal translocations, uterine structural abnormalities (septum, fibroids, adhesions), antiphospholipid antibody syndrome, SLE, thyroid disease, and diabetes.

front_text: Therapeutic abortion is an induced abortion performed because the mother's life or health is endangered, or the fetus is nonviable (e.g., lethal malformations, fetal demise).
front_html: Therapeutic abortion is an induced abortion performed because the mother's life or health is {{c1::endangered}}, or the fetus is {{c1::nonviable}} (e.g., lethal malformations, fetal demise).
extra: Other induced abortion type: Elective abortion = performed for personal or social reasons in the absence of medical indications.
vignette: You are seeing Abortion Annie, a 28-year-old woman presenting to your outpatient obstetrics clinic after a confirmed intrauterine pregnancy at 8 weeks by last menstrual period (LMP). She reports vaginal bleeding and lower abdominal cramping that began 24 hours ago, and she is asking about her options and what her diagnosis means. You explain that pregnancy loss before 20 weeks gestation is classified as an abortion, while loss at or after 20 weeks is classified as a fetal demise or stillbirth. Her loss at 8 weeks falls within the early abortion category, defined as loss before 12 weeks, as opposed to late abortion, which occurs between 12 and 20 weeks. As a PA, always anchor your classification of pregnancy loss to gestational age by LMP, because the 20-week threshold determines whether you are managing an abortion, a fetal demise, or a preterm delivery, and this distinction drives every subsequent clinical and legal decision.
teaching_case:
Patient Presentation
You are seeing Abortion Annie, a 28-year-old woman presenting to your outpatient obstetrics clinic with vaginal bleeding and pelvic cramping at 8 weeks gestation by LMP, requesting clarification of her diagnosis and options. She has a confirmed intrauterine pregnancy on prior ultrasound and denies fever, shoulder pain, or syncope. She asks whether her situation is considered a miscarriage, a stillbirth, or something else, and whether it was caused by anything she did. You explain that abortion is defined as any pregnancy loss before 20 weeks gestation by LMP, encompassing both spontaneous (natural) and induced (intentional) losses, and that her presentation at 8 weeks is classified as an early abortion (before 12 weeks), distinct from a late abortion (12 to 20 weeks) or a fetal demise (at or after 20 weeks).

Physical Examination
On examination, her vital signs are stable with no signs of hemodynamic compromise. Speculum examination reveals a small amount of blood in the vaginal vault with a closed cervical os, which is consistent with a threatened or incomplete abortion depending on subsequent imaging. Bimanual examination confirms a uterus consistent with early pregnancy without adnexal tenderness or mass, making ectopic pregnancy less likely. Pertinent negatives include absence of peritoneal signs and no cervical motion tenderness.

Workup and Diagnosis
You order a transvaginal ultrasound, which is the cornerstone of abortion evaluation, to assess for gestational sac location, fetal cardiac activity, and uterine contents. A quantitative β-hCG is drawn; in a viable pregnancy, β-hCG should double every 48 hours, and a plateau or decline raises concern for miscarriage or ectopic pregnancy. Blood type and Rh status are checked to determine whether Rho(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM) is indicated to prevent Rh sensitization. The classification of the abortion as spontaneous versus induced and early versus late is confirmed by gestational age on ultrasound correlated with LMP.

Treatment
Management is tailored to the type and classification of abortion identified. For a spontaneous early abortion, options include expectant management (allowing natural passage of products of conception), medical management with misoprostol to induce uterine contractions, or surgical management with suction curettage or D&C for retained products or heavy bleeding. For an induced abortion, the method selected depends on gestational age: medical abortion with mifepristone plus misoprostol is used up to 7 to 9 weeks LMP, while surgical options are used for later gestations. Rho(D) immune globulin is administered to all Rh-negative patients regardless of abortion type.

Follow Up and Monitoring
You schedule follow-up with serial β-hCG or repeat transvaginal ultrasound to confirm complete passage of products of conception and resolution of pregnancy tissue. You provide emotional support and preconception counseling, including discussion of future pregnancy planning and any modifiable risk factors.

PA EOR Board Pearls
1. Abortion is defined as pregnancy loss before 20 weeks gestation by LMP; loss at or after 20 weeks is classified as fetal demise or stillbirth.
2. Early abortion occurs before 12 weeks; late abortion occurs between 12 and 20 weeks; a fetus born alive between 20 and 36 weeks 6 days is a preterm delivery.
3. Spontaneous abortion is noninduced pregnancy loss; therapeutic abortion is induced due to maternal health risk or fetal nonviability; elective abortion is induced for personal or social reasons.
4. Always check blood type and Rh status in any abortion; administer RhoGAM to all Rh-negative patients to prevent isoimmunization.
5. Transvaginal ultrasound is the gold standard imaging modality for evaluating any type of abortion and confirming gestational age and uterine contents.

teaching_case:
Patient Presentation
You are seeing Spontaneous Sam, a 37-year-old woman presenting to your urgent care clinic at 9 weeks gestation by LMP with vaginal bleeding and pelvic cramping and a history of two prior spontaneous abortions. She has poorly controlled type 2 diabetes, smokes actively, and her partner is 47 years old, all of which are recognized risk factors for spontaneous abortion. She denies fever, shoulder pain, or syncope, and her vital signs are stable. Approximately 60% of spontaneous abortions are caused by chromosomal abnormalities, most commonly trisomy 16, and most occur in the first trimester.

Physical Examination
On speculum examination you identify blood in the vaginal vault with a closed cervical os, consistent with a threatened or missed abortion. Bimanual examination reveals a uterus appropriate for gestational age without adnexal tenderness or mass, making ectopic pregnancy less likely. Pertinent negatives include no cervical motion tenderness and no peritoneal signs. The closed os in the setting of confirmed fetal demise on ultrasound is consistent with a missed abortion.

Workup and Diagnosis
You order a transvaginal ultrasound, which demonstrates absent fetal cardiac activity and confirms intrauterine pregnancy, ruling out ectopic pregnancy. A quantitative β-hCG drawn 48 hours apart shows a plateau rather than the expected doubling, confirming nonviable pregnancy. Blood type and Rh status are checked; she is Rh-negative, so Rho(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM) is indicated. Because this is her third pregnancy loss, you initiate a recurrent abortion workup including parental karyotyping, thyroid function tests, and antiphospholipid antibody testing to evaluate for genetic, endocrine, and autoimmune causes including antiphospholipid antibody syndrome and SLE.

You discuss three management options with the patient: expectant management (allowing natural passage of products of conception with monitoring), medical management with misoprostol (a prostaglandin that induces uterine contractions), or surgical management with suction curettage or D&C, which is indicated for retained products of conception or heavy bleeding. She opts for medical management, and you administer misoprostol with instructions for home use and return precautions. Rho(D) immune globulin is administered given her Rh-negative status.

You schedule follow-up with serial β-hCG and repeat transvaginal ultrasound to confirm complete passage of products of conception and return of β-hCG to nonpregnant levels. You provide emotional support, preconception counseling, and review results of the recurrent abortion workup to guide management of future pregnancies.

1. Approximately 60% of spontaneous abortions are caused by chromosomal abnormalities; the most common specific chromosomal cause is trisomy 16.
2. Risk factors for spontaneous abortion include advanced maternal age (≥35 years), paternal age ≥45, smoking, alcohol, cocaine use, and poorly controlled diabetes, thyroid disease, and hypertension.

4. In a viable pregnancy, β-hCG doubles every 48 hours; a plateau or decline indicates miscarriage or ectopic pregnancy.
5. Transvaginal ultrasound is the cornerstone of abortion evaluation; key findings include empty uterus, abnormal gestational sac, and absent fetal cardiac activity.
6. Always check Rh status and administer RhoGAM to Rh-negative patients with any pregnancy loss to prevent isoimmunization.
7. Causes of recurrent abortion include parental chromosomal translocations, uterine structural abnormalities (septum, fibroids, adhesions), antiphospholipid antibody syndrome, SLE, thyroid disease, and diabetes.

front_text: Medical abortion uses mifepristone combined with misoprostol and is indicated up to 7–9 weeks LMP.
front_html: Medical abortion uses {{c1::mifepristone}} combined with {{c1::misoprostol}} and is indicated up to {{c1::7–9}} weeks LMP.
extra:
Other elective abortion methods:
•Suction curettage (manual vacuum aspiration) = safest and most effective for ≤12 weeks LMP, performed with local anesthesia. •D&C = surgical abortion up to 16 weeks LMP. •D&E = used for 14–18 weeks gestation, outpatient surgical procedure. •Induction of labor = after 16 weeks, using vaginal prostaglandins or intraamniotic saline/urea
typically for fetal demise or lethal anomalies.
vignette: You are seeing Induced Ivy, a 24-year-old woman presenting to your reproductive health clinic at 8 weeks gestation by LMP requesting an elective induced abortion. She is otherwise healthy, her intrauterine pregnancy is confirmed by transvaginal ultrasound, and she has no contraindications to medical management. You counsel her that at 8 weeks she is a candidate for medical abortion with mifepristone followed by misoprostol, where mifepristone blocks progesterone and misoprostol induces uterine contractions to expel the pregnancy. You explain that suction curettage (manual vacuum aspiration) is the safest and most effective surgical option for gestations up to 12 weeks LMP and is performed under local anesthesia, while dilation and evacuation (D&E) is used for gestations of 14 to 18 weeks. As a PA, always match the abortion method to gestational age by LMP, because each method has a defined gestational window and selecting outside that window increases the risk of incomplete abortion, hemorrhage, and procedural complications.
teaching_case:
Patient Presentation
You are seeing Induced Ivy, a 24-year-old woman presenting to your reproductive health clinic at 8 weeks gestation by LMP requesting an elective induced abortion for personal reasons, with no medical indications or contraindications identified. Her intrauterine pregnancy is confirmed by transvaginal ultrasound, and she is hemodynamically stable with no signs of infection or ectopic pregnancy. She has no history of coagulopathy, adrenal insufficiency, or long-term corticosteroid use, which would be contraindications to mifepristone. She asks you to explain all available options and their gestational age limits so she can make an informed decision.

Physical Examination
Vital signs are normal and she appears well. Speculum examination reveals a normal cervix without lesions or discharge, and bimanual examination confirms a uterus consistent with 8 weeks gestation without adnexal tenderness or mass. There are no signs of pelvic inflammatory disease or active cervical infection, which would require treatment prior to any surgical procedure. Pertinent negatives include no cervical motion tenderness and no adnexal fullness.

Workup and Diagnosis
You confirm gestational age by transvaginal ultrasound and verify intrauterine location of the pregnancy. Blood type and Rh status are checked; Rho(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM) is administered if she is Rh-negative. A complete blood count is obtained to assess baseline hemoglobin prior to the procedure. At 8 weeks LMP, she is within the window for medical abortion with mifepristone plus misoprostol (indicated up to 7 to 9 weeks LMP) as well as suction curettage (indicated up to 12 weeks LMP).

Treatment
After shared decision making, she chooses medical abortion: mifepristone is administered first to block progesterone receptors and sensitize the uterus, followed 24 to 48 hours later by misoprostol, a prostaglandin that induces uterine contractions and cervical softening to expel the pregnancy. If she had chosen surgical management, suction curettage (manual vacuum aspiration) would be the preferred method at this gestational age, performed under local anesthesia as the safest and most effective option up to 12 weeks LMP. For gestations of 14 to 18 weeks, dilation and evacuation (D&E) is the appropriate outpatient surgical method, while induction of labor with vaginal prostaglandins or intraamniotic saline or urea is used after 16 weeks, typically for fetal demise or lethal anomalies.

Follow Up and Monitoring
You schedule follow-up in 1 to 2 weeks with a repeat transvaginal ultrasound or serial β-hCG to confirm complete expulsion of the pregnancy and return of β-hCG to nonpregnant levels. You counsel her on expected symptoms including cramping and bleeding, red flag signs requiring emergency evaluation such as soaking more than two pads per hour or fever, and discuss contraception options going forward.

PA EOR Board Pearls
1. Medical abortion with mifepristone plus misoprostol is indicated up to 7 to 9 weeks LMP; mifepristone blocks progesterone and misoprostol causes uterine contractions.
2. Suction curettage (manual vacuum aspiration) is the safest and most effective surgical abortion method for gestations up to 12 weeks LMP and is performed under local anesthesia.
3. Dilation and curettage (D&C) is used for elective abortion up to 16 weeks LMP; dilation and evacuation (D&E) is used for gestations of 14 to 18 weeks.
4. Induction of labor as an abortion method is used after 16 weeks with vaginal prostaglandins or intraamniotic saline or urea, typically for fetal demise or lethal anomalies.
5. Always administer Rho(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM) to Rh-negative patients undergoing any induced abortion to prevent isoimmunization.
6. Confirm intrauterine location by transvaginal ultrasound before any abortion procedure to exclude ectopic pregnancy.

front_text: In medical abortion, mifepristone blocks progesterone, while misoprostol causes uterine contractions.
front_html: In medical abortion, {{c1::mifepristone}} blocks {{c1::progesterone}}, while {{c1::misoprostol}} causes uterine {{c1::contractions}}.
vignette: You are seeing Induced Ivy, a 24-year-old woman presenting to your reproductive health clinic at 8 weeks gestation by LMP requesting an elective induced abortion. She is otherwise healthy, her intrauterine pregnancy is confirmed by transvaginal ultrasound, and she has no contraindications to medical management. You counsel her that at 8 weeks she is a candidate for medical abortion with mifepristone followed by misoprostol, where mifepristone blocks progesterone and misoprostol induces uterine contractions to expel the pregnancy. You explain that suction curettage (manual vacuum aspiration) is the safest and most effective surgical option for gestations up to 12 weeks LMP and is performed under local anesthesia, while dilation and evacuation (D&E) is used for gestations of 14 to 18 weeks. As a PA, always match the abortion method to gestational age by LMP, because each method has a defined gestational window and selecting outside that window increases the risk of incomplete abortion, hemorrhage, and procedural complications.
teaching_case:
Patient Presentation
You are seeing Induced Ivy, a 24-year-old woman presenting to your reproductive health clinic at 8 weeks gestation by LMP requesting an elective induced abortion for personal reasons, with no medical indications or contraindications identified. She has no history of coagulopathy, adrenal insufficiency, or long-term corticosteroid use, which would be contraindications to mifepristone. She asks you to explain all available options and their gestational age limits so she can make an informed decision.

Physical Examination
Vital signs are normal and she appears well. Speculum examination reveals a normal cervix without lesions or discharge, and bimanual examination confirms a uterus consistent with 8 weeks gestation without adnexal tenderness or mass. There are no signs of pelvic inflammatory disease or active cervical infection, which would require treatment prior to any surgical procedure. Pertinent negatives include no cervical motion tenderness and no adnexal fullness.

Workup and Diagnosis
You confirm gestational age by transvaginal ultrasound and verify intrauterine location of the pregnancy. Blood type and Rh status are checked; Rho(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM) is administered if she is Rh-negative. A complete blood count is obtained to assess baseline hemoglobin prior to the procedure. At 8 weeks LMP, she is within the window for medical abortion with mifepristone plus misoprostol (indicated up to 7 to 9 weeks LMP) as well as suction curettage (indicated up to 12 weeks LMP).

Treatment
After shared decision making, she chooses medical abortion: mifepristone is administered first to block progesterone receptors and sensitize the uterus, followed 24 to 48 hours later by misoprostol, a prostaglandin that induces uterine contractions and cervical softening to expel the pregnancy. If she had chosen surgical management, suction curettage (manual vacuum aspiration) would be the preferred method at this gestational age, performed under local anesthesia as the safest and most effective option up to 12 weeks LMP. For gestations of 14 to 18 weeks, dilation and evacuation (D&E) is the appropriate outpatient surgical method, while induction of labor with vaginal prostaglandins or intraamniotic saline or urea is used after 16 weeks, typically for fetal demise or lethal anomalies.

Follow Up and Monitoring
You schedule follow-up in 1 to 2 weeks with a repeat transvaginal ultrasound or serial β-hCG to confirm complete expulsion of the pregnancy and return of β-hCG to nonpregnant levels. You counsel her on expected symptoms including cramping and bleeding, red flag signs requiring emergency evaluation such as soaking more than two pads per hour or fever, and discuss contraception options going forward.
